question:
Suppose that a sequence $a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots$ satisfies
$0 < a_n \leq a_{2n} + a_{2n+1}$ for all $n \geq 1$. Prove that the series
$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$ diverges.
solution:
Solution. For \( m \geq 1 \), let \( b_{m}=\sum_{i=2^{m-1}}^{2} a_{i} \). Summing \( a_{n} \leq a_{2 n}+a_{2 n+1} \) from \( n=2^{m-1} \) to \( n=2^{m}-1 \) yields \( b_{m} \leq b_{m+1} \) for all \( m \geq 1 \). For any \( t \geq 1 \),
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{2^{t}-1} a_{n}=\sum_{m=1}^{t} b_{m} \geq t b_{1}=t a_{1}
\]
which is unbounded as \( t \rightarrow \infty \) since \( a_{1}>0 \), so \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_{n} \) diverges.
Alternatively, assuming the series converges to a finite value \( L \), we obtain the contradiction
\[
\begin{aligned}
L & =b_{1}+\left(b_{2}+b_{3}+\cdots\right) \\
& \geq b_{1}+\left(b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots\right) \\
& =b_{1}+L
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Suppose that a sequence $firstterm, a_2, a_3, \dots$ satisfies
$0 < seqterm \leq seqdouble + seqdoubleplus$ for all $indexn \geq 1$. Prove that the series
$\sum_{indexn=1}^{\infty} seqterm$ diverges.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. For $( levelm \geq 1 )$, let $ blocksum=\sum_{indexi=2^{levelm-1}}^{2} seqindex $. Summing $ seqterm \leq seqdouble+seqdoubleplus $ from $ indexn=2^{levelm-1} $ to $ indexn=2^{levelm}-1 $ yields $ blocksum \leq blocksumnext $ for all $ levelm \geq 1 $. For any $ levelt \geq 1 $,
\[
\sum_{indexn=1}^{2^{levelt}-1} seqterm=\sum_{levelm=1}^{levelt} blocksum \geq levelt\, blocksumone=levelt\, firstterm
\]
which is unbounded as $ levelt \rightarrow \infty $ since $ firstterm>0 $, so $ \sum_{indexn=1}^{\infty} seqterm $ diverges.
Alternatively, assuming the series converges to a finite value $ limitval $, we obtain the contradiction
\[
\begin{aligned}
limitval & =blocksumone+\left(blocksumtwo+blocksumthree+\cdots\right) \\
& \geq blocksumone+\left(blocksumone+blocksumtwo+\cdots\right) \\
& =blocksumone+limitval
\end{aligned}
\]
which is impossible; therefore the series cannot converge.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Suppose that a sequence $strawberry, a_2, a_3, \dots$ satisfies
$0 < blueberry \leq raspberry + gooseberry$ for all $sunflower \geq 1$. Prove that the series
$\sum_{sunflower=1}^{\infty} blueberry$ diverges.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. For \( pancake \geq 1 \), let \( marshmallow=\sum_{wardrobe=2^{pancake-1}}^{2} cranberry \). Summing \( blueberry \leq raspberry+gooseberry \) from \( sunflower=2^{pancake-1} \) to \( sunflower=2^{pancake}-1 \) yields \( marshmallow \leq butterscotch \) for all \( pancake \geq 1 \). For any \( lemonade \geq 1 \),
\[
\sum_{sunflower=1}^{2^{lemonade}-1} blueberry=\sum_{pancake=1}^{lemonade} marshmallow \geq lemonade\; chocolate=lemonade\; strawberry
\]
which is unbounded as \( lemonade \rightarrow \infty \) since \( strawberry>0 \), so \( \sum_{sunflower=1}^{\infty} blueberry \) diverges.
Alternatively, assuming the series converges to a finite value \( buttermilk \), we obtain the contradiction
\[
\begin{aligned}
buttermilk & =chocolate+\left(cheesecake+moussecake+\cdots\right) \\
& \geq chocolate+\left(chocolate+cheesecake+\cdots\right) \\
& =chocolate+buttermilk
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Suppose that a sequence $zerosequenceone, a_2, a_3, \dots$ satisfies
$0 < zerosequence \leq zerosequenceeven + zerosequenceodd$ for all $continuum \geq 1$. Prove that the series
$\sum_{continuum=1}^{\infty} zerosequence$ diverges.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. For \( fraction \geq 1 \), let \( shrinksum=\sum_{outsider=2^{fraction-1}}^{2} zerosequenceindex \). Summing \( zerosequence \leq zerosequenceeven+zerosequenceodd \) from \( continuum=2^{fraction-1} \) to \( continuum=2^{fraction}-1 \) yields \( shrinksum \leq shrinksumnext \) for all \( fraction \geq 1 \). For any \( staticvar \geq 1 \),
\[
\sum_{continuum=1}^{2^{staticvar}-1} zerosequence=\sum_{fraction=1}^{staticvar} shrinksum \geq staticvar shrinksumone=staticvar zerosequenceone
\]
which is unbounded as \( staticvar \rightarrow \infty \) since \( zerosequenceone>0 \), so \( \sum_{continuum=1}^{\infty} zerosequence \) diverges.
Alternatively, assuming the series converges to a finite value \( infinityvalue \), we obtain the contradiction
\[
\begin{aligned}
infinityvalue & =shrinksumone+\left(shrinksumtwo+shrinksumthree+\cdots\right) \\
& \geq shrinksumone+\left(shrinksumone+shrinksumtwo+\cdots\right) \\
& =shrinksumone+infinityvalue
\end{aligned}
\]

garbled_string.question:
Suppose that a sequence $icxewpno, a_2, a_3, \dots$ satisfies
$0 < kzfdyniv \leq ysqplmre + wdkunbza$ for all $qzxwvtnp \geq 1$. Prove that the series
$\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{\infty} kzfdyniv$ diverges.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. For \( ghsdaluy \geq 1 \), let \( pahokzdr=\sum_{ncewyprk=2^{ghsdaluy-1}}^{2} rhtgvope \). Summing \( kzfdyniv \leq ysqplmre+wdkunbza \) from \( qzxwvtnp=2^{ghsdaluy-1} \) to \( qzxwvtnp=2^{ghsdaluy}-1 \) yields \( pahokzdr \leq xjtwlqse \) for all \( ghsdaluy \geq 1 \). For any \( vnmhqdse \geq 1 \),
\[
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{2^{vnmhqdse}-1} kzfdyniv=\sum_{ghsdaluy=1}^{vnmhqdse} pahokzdr \geq vnmhqdse\, qudskvya=vnmhqdse\, icxewpno
\]
which is unbounded as \( vnmhqdse \rightarrow \infty \) since \( icxewpno>0 \), so \( \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{\infty} kzfdyniv \) diverges.
Alternatively, assuming the series converges to a finite value \( oemrwlyg \), we obtain the contradiction
\[
\begin{aligned}
oemrwlyg & =qudskvya+\left(muycdahn+fzarlbqe+\cdots\right) \\
& \geq qudskvya+\left(qudskvya+muycdahn+\cdots\right) \\
& =qudskvya+oemrwlyg
\end{aligned}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $(a_n)_{n\ge 1}$ be a sequence of positive real numbers satisfying
\[
0< a_n\le a_{3n}+a_{3n+1}+a_{3n+2}\qquad\text{for every }n\ge 1.
\]
Show that the series
\[\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n\]
diverges.
kernel_variant.solution:
For each integer m \geq  1 define the ``block-sum''

b_m := \sum _{i=3^{m-1}}^{3^m-1} a_i.

(Thus b_1 = a_1 + a_2, b_2 = a_3 + a_4 + \cdots  + a_8, and so on.)

Step 1. Monotonicity of the block sums.
Fix m \geq  1 and sum the given inequality for all n with 3^{m-1} \leq  n \leq  3^m-1:

\sum _{n=3^{m-1}}^{3^m-1} a_n \leq  \sum _{n=3^{m-1}}^{3^m-1} (a_{3n} + a_{3n+1} + a_{3n+2}).

As n runs from 3^{m-1} to 3^m-1, the triples (3n,3n+1,3n+2) enumerate exactly the integers j=3^m,\ldots ,3^{m+1}-1 once each. Hence the right-hand side is

    \sum _{j=3^m}^{3^{m+1}-1} a_j = b_{m+1}.

Therefore

    b_m \leq  b_{m+1}

for all m \geq  1, so the sequence (b_m) is nondecreasing.

Step 2. A lower bound for partial sums.
For any integer t \geq  1, the first 3^t-1 terms of the series split into the first t blocks:

    \sum _{n=1}^{3^t-1} a_n = \sum _{m=1}^t b_m \geq  t \cdot  b_1.

Here b_1 = a_1 + a_2 > 0 because all a_n are positive.

Step 3. Divergence of the series.
Since b_1 > 0, t\cdot b_1 \to  \infty  as t \to  \infty . Thus the partial sums \sum _{n=1}^{3^t-1} a_n are unbounded, and so \sum _{n=1}^\infty  a_n diverges.

core_steps: Group the sequence into consecutive ‘blocks’ whose indices run from 2^{m-1} to 2^{m}-1; call their sums b_m.
Add the given inequality over one block to get b_m ≤ b_{m+1}; hence the block sums form a non-decreasing sequence.
Express the partial sum up to 2^{t}-1 as Σ_{m=1}^{t} b_m and bound it below by t·b_1.
Because b_1 = a_1 > 0, these partial sums grow without bound, so the series ∑ a_n diverges.

slot1.description: Branching/base number that determines both the size of each successive block and the indices on the right-hand side of the inequality.

slot2.description: Choice of the initial positive term used to obtain the linear lower bound t·b_1 in the partial sums.
